title: Time conversion word problems
description: Solve word problems to find what time an event occurred or how long an event lasted.

assessment_items.question_content:
It takes Ebru $13$ minutes to bike to school.  If she walks, it takes her twice as long. Ebru leaves for school at $7{:}15$.

**What time will Ebru get to school if she walks?**

[[☃ input-number 1]] ${:}$ [[☃ input-number 2]]
hints:
It takes Ebru $\blueD{13}$ minutes to bike to school, and she walks there in $\greenD{\text{twice}}$ that time.

$\blueD{13}\text{ min} \times\greenD2= \purpleD{26}\text{ min}$

It takes Ebru $\purpleD{26}$ minutes to walk to school.
Ebru will get to school at $7{:}41$.

assessment_items.question_content:
It takes Bob $36$ minutes to walk to school.  If he runs, it takes him half that time. Bob leaves for school at $7{:}27$.

**What time will Bob get to school if he runs?**

[[☃ input-number 1]] : [[☃ input-number 2]]
hints:
It takes Bob $\blueD{36}$ minutes to walk to school. He can run there in $\maroonD{\text{half}}$ of that time.

$\blueD{36}\text{ minutes} \times\maroonD{\dfrac12} = \greenD{18}\text{ minutes}$

It takes Bob $\greenD{18}$ minutes to run to school.
Bob will get to school at $7{:}{45}$.

assessment_items.question_content:
It takes Natasha $33$ minutes to walk to the pool from her house.  If she rides her bike, it takes her $\dfrac13$ of that time. 

**Natasha leaves her house for the pool at $3{:}12$. If she is riding her bike, what time will she arrive?**

[[☃ input-number 1]] : [[☃ input-number 2]]
hints:
Biking to the pool takes $\maroonD{\dfrac13}$ as long as walking does. Since walking takes $\blueD{33}$ minutes:

$\blueD{33}\text{ minutes} \times\maroonD{\dfrac13} = \greenD{11}\text{ minutes}$
Natasha will arrive at the pool at $3{:}23$.

assessment_items.question_content:
It takes Chris $30$ minutes to walk to work.  If he drives, it takes him $\dfrac13$ of that time. 

**What is the latest time that Chris can leave to drive to work and arrive by $8{:}00$?**

[[☃ input-number 1]] : [[☃ input-number 2]]
hints:
Driving to work takes $\maroonD{\dfrac13}$ as long as walking does. Since walking takes $\blueD{30}$ minutes:

$\blueD{30}\text{ minutes} \times\maroonD{\dfrac13} = \purpleD{10}\text{ minutes}$

It takes Chris $\purpleD{10}$ minutes to drive to work.

Chris needs to arrive at work by  $8{:}00$, and it takes him $\purpleD{10}$ minutes to drive there.

[[☃ image 1]]

$8{:}00 - \purpleD{10}\text{ minutes} = 7{:}50$
Chris has to leave by $7{:}50$ to get to work on time.

assessment_items.question_content:
Wilma loves to run.  Wilma ran for $1$ hour and $15$ minutes $3$ days this week.

**How many minutes did Wilma run in total?**    

[[☃ input-number 1]] minutes

[[☃ explanation 1]]
hints:
Wilma runs $\purpleD{75}$ minutes on $\greenD3$ days.  Let's multiply to find the total.

$\purpleD{75} \times \greenD3= \text{?}$
Wilma ran for a total of $225$ minutes.

Conversions| | | |||
- | | | |
$1$ kilometer $= 1{,}000$ meters| | $1$ minute $= 60$ seconds|| $1$ liter$= 1{,}000$ milliliters|
$1$ meter $= 100$ centimeter| |$1$ hour $= 60$ minutes|| $1$ kilogram $= 1{,}000$ grams|

$1$ centimeter $= 10$ millimeters|| |||

assessment_items.question_content:
Ajay is allowed to watch $2$ hours of television on Saturdays.  Each episode of his favorite show lasts $30$ minutes.

**How many episodes may he watch?**  

[[☃ input-number 1]] episodes

[[☃ explanation 1]]
hints:
Next, we can divide the $\greenD{120}$ minutes into pieces that are each $\maroonD{30}$ minutes long.

How many $\maroonD{30}$ minute shows does it take to make $\greenD{120}$ minutes?

$\greenD{120}\text{ minutes} \div \maroonD{30}\text{ minutes} = \text{?}$
Ajay can watch $4$ episodes of his favorite show.

assessment_items.question_content:
Ronald is making pot roast for dinner.  He has been cooking the pot roast for $1$ hour and $5$ minutes.  The pot roast needs to cook for $40$ more minutes.

**How many minutes does the pot roast cook in total?**

[[☃ input-number 1]] minutes

[[☃ explanation 1]]
hints:
The pot roast has been cooking for $65$ minutes, and it still needs to cook for $40$ minutes.

$65$ minutes $+$ $40$ minutes $=\text{?}$ minutes

[[☃ image 1]]
The pot roast needs to cook for a total  of $105$ minutes.

assessment_items.question_content:
Duane is making a casserole for dinner.  He has been cooking the casserole for $1$ hour and $48$ minutes.  The casserole needs to cook for $47$ more minutes.

**How many minutes does the casserole cook in total?**

[[☃ input-number 1]] minutes

[[☃ explanation 1]]
hints:
First we need to convert $\blueD1$ hour $\greenD{48}$ minutes.

$1$ hour $= \blueD{60}$ minutes

$\begin{align} 
\blueD1\text{ hour }\greenD{48}\text{ minutes} &=\blueD{60}\text{ minutes}+\greenD{48}\text{ minutes}\\\\
&=108\text{ minutes}\end{align}$

The casserole has been cooking for $108$ minutes, and it still needs to cook for $47$ minutes.

$108 + 47 =\text{?}$

[[☃ image 1]]
The casserole needs to cook for a total of $155$ minutes.

assessment_items.question_content:
Alma wants to swim one lap in the pool as fast as her friend Blanca, who can swim one lap in $1$ minute and $23$ seconds. Alma started swimming her lap in the pool $57$ seconds ago. 

**How much time does Alma have left to finish her lap if she wants to tie Blanca's time?**  
*Write the answer in seconds.*

[[☃ input-number 1]] seconds

[[☃ explanation 1]]
hints:
Alma has been swimming for $57$ seconds, and she has to finish in $83$ seconds.  

$57 + \text{?} = 83$  
$83 - 57 = \text{?}$


[[☃ image 1]]
Alma has $26$ seconds to finish her lap.

assessment_items.question_content:
Shelbi wants to run one lap around the track as fast as her friend Annabelle, who can run one lap in $1$ minute and $10$ seconds. Shelbi started running her lap around the track $47$ seconds ago. 

**How much time does Shelbi have left to finish her lap if she wants to tie Annabelle's time?**  
*Write the answer in seconds.*

[[☃ input-number 1]] seconds

[[☃ explanation 1]]
hints:
Shelbi has been running for $47$ seconds, and she has to finish in $70$ seconds.  

$47 + \text{?} = 70$  
$70 - 47 = \text{?}$

[[☃ image 1]]
Shelbi has $23$ seconds to finish her lap.

assessment_items.question_content:
Walter is making curry for dinner.  The recipe says that the curry should cook for $1$ hour and $25$ minutes.  He has been cooking the curry for $59$ minutes.  

**According to the recipe, how much longer does Walter need to cook the curry?**  
*Write the answer in minutes.*

[[☃ input-number 1]] minutes

[[☃ explanation 1]]
hints:
So the curry needs to cook for $85$ minutes, and it has been cooking for $59$ minutes.  

$59 + \text{?} = 85$  
$85 - 59 = \text{?}$

[[☃ image 1]]
Walter has to continue cooking the curry for $26$ minutes.

assessment_items.question_content:
Martin is taking a test at school.  His teacher gave him $1$ hour and $15$ minutes to complete the test.  He has been taking the test for $54$ minutes.  

**How many minutes does Martin have left to finish the test?**

[[☃ input-number 1]] minutes

[[☃ explanation 1]]
hints:
First we need to convert $\blueD1$ hour and $\greenD{15}$ minutes. 

$1$ hour $= \blueD{60}$ minutes

$\begin{align} 
\blueD1\text{ hour }\greenD{15}\text{ minutes} &=\blueD{60}\text{ minutes}+\greenD{15}\text{ minutes}
\\\\
&=75\text{ minutes}\end{align}$
Martin has $75$ minutes to take the test, and he has been taking the test for $54$ minutes.  

$54 + \text{?} = 75$  
$75 - 54= \text{?}$

[[☃ image 1]]

Martin has $21$ minutes left to finish the test.

assessment_items.question_content:
Amanda is cooking a turkey for dinner.  The recipe says that the turkey should cook for $1\dfrac12$ hours.  She has been cooking the turkey for $25$ minutes.  

**According to the recipe, how many more minutes does the turkey need to cook?**

[[☃ input-number 1]] minutes

[[☃ explanation 1]]
hints:
First we need to convert $\blueD{1}\greenD{\dfrac12}$ hours.

$1$ hour $= \blueD{60}$ minutes

$\begin{align} 
\blueD1\greenD{\dfrac12}\text{ hour }&=\blueD{60}\text{ minutes}+\greenD{30}\text{ minutes}\\\\
&=90\text{ minutes}\end{align}$
The turkey needs to cook for $90$ minutes, and it has been cooking for $25$ minutes.  

$25 + \text{?} = 90$  
$90 - 25= \text{?}$

[[☃ image 1]]

Amanda has to cook the turkey for $65$ minutes more.

assessment_items.question_content:
It takes Carrie $58$ minutes to get home. 

**If Carrie leaves for home at $1{:}32$, what time will she arrive?**

[[☃ input-number 1]] : [[☃ input-number 2]]
hints:
We start at $1{:}32$ when Carrie leaves her home.

[[☃ image 1]]

To determine what time Carrie will arrive, we go **right** ${58}$ minutes from $1{:}32$.

[[☃ image 1]]

$\begin{align}
{1{:}32} +{} &\maroonD{8}\text{ minutes} = 1{:}40\\\\
{1{:}40} +{} &\purpleD{20}\text{ minutes} = {2{:}00}\\\\
2{:}00 +{} &\blueD{30}\text{ minutes} = {2{:}30}
\end{align}$
Carrie will arrive home at $2{:}30$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Bart's drive to the airport takes $23$ minutes. He needs to arrive at the airport by a quarter to $6$.

**If Bart needs to arrive by a quarter before $6$, what is the latest time Bart can leave for the airport?**

[[☃ input-number 1]] : [[☃ input-number 2]]
hints:
Bart has to be at the airport by a quarter before $6$. What time is a quarter before $6$?

$1$ hour $= 60$ minutes  

$\begin{align}
1\text{ quarter hour} &= 60 \times \dfrac{1}{4} \\\\
&=\dfrac{60\times1}{4}\\\\
&=60\div4\\\\
&= 15\text{ minutes}
\end{align}$

A quarter before $6$ is $5{:}45$.

So, we start at $5{:}45$ when Bart needs to arrive at the airport.

[[☃ image 1]]

To determine what time Bart will need to leave, we go **left** ${23}$ minutes from $5{:}45$.

[[☃ image 1]]

$\begin{align}
{5{:}45} -{} &\maroonD{15}\text{ minutes} = 5{:}30\\\\
{5{:}30} -{} &\purpleD{5}\text{ minutes} = {5{:}25}\\\\
5{:}25 -{} &\blueD{3}\text{ minutes} = {5{:}22}
\end{align}$
Bart must leave by $5{:}22$ in order to get to the airport by a quarter before $6$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Max's trip home takes $32$ minutes.  He needs to arrive home by half past $5$.

**What is the latest time Max can leave to be home?**

[[☃ input-number 1]] : [[☃ input-number 2]]
hints:
Max has to be home half past $5$.  What time is half past $5$?

$1$ hour $= 60$ minutes  

$\begin{align}
\dfrac12\text{ hour} &= 60 \times \dfrac{1}{2} \\\\
&=\dfrac{60\times1}{2}\\\\
&=60\div2\\\\
&= 30\text{ minutes}
\end{align}$

Half past $5$ is $5{:}30$.

So, we start at $5{:}30$ when Max needs to arrive home.


[[☃ image 1]]
Max must leave by $4{:}58$ in order to get home by half past $5$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Mr. Potter's class will take a test for $1\dfrac12$ hours.  They need to finish by half past $11$ to get to lunch on time.

**What is the latest time the class can start the test to get to lunch on time?**

[[☃ input-number 1]] : [[☃ input-number 2]]
hints:
Mr. Potter's class needs to be done at half past $11$.  What time is half past $11$?

$1$ hour $= 60$ minutes  

$\begin{align}
\dfrac12\text{ hour} &= 60 \times \dfrac{1}{2} \\\\
&=\dfrac{60\times1}{2}\\\\
&=60\div2\\\\
&= 30\text{ minutes}
\end{align}$

Half past $11$ is $11{:}30$.

So, we start at $11{:}30$ when Mr. Potter's class needs to go to lunch.

[[☃ image 1]]
Mr. Potter's class should start the test no later than $10{:}00$.
